questions.text: Muslim scientists made important discoveries in the period:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This time frame broadly covers the Islamic Golden Age, which is considered to have begun in the 8th century and extended into the 14th century. During this period, Muslim scientists, scholars, and intellectuals made significant contributions to various fields, including mathematics, astronomy, medicine, chemistry, physics, and philosophy. Scholars from the Islamic world preserved and built upon the knowledge of ancient civilizations, particularly Greek and Roman, and made groundbreaking discoveries of their own.

While the Islamic Golden Age is an important part of this time frame, it also extends beyond the traditional end of the Golden Age. The latter part of this period saw a decline in scientific and intellectual activity in the Islamic world, and it corresponds more closely to the decline of the Abbasid Caliphate and the fragmentation of political and cultural unity. However, some scientific and philosophical activities continued in various regions.

This time frame predates the Islamic Golden Age and encompasses the early period of Islamic civilization. While there were important developments in the sciences during this time, the peak of scientific achievement in the Islamic world occurred later, particularly during the Abbasid Caliphate.

This option covers the period that saw the rise of the Islamic civilization and the early stages of the Islamic Golden Age. The Abbasid Caliphate, established in 750 AD, played a crucial role in fostering an environment that encouraged scientific and intellectual pursuits. The House of Wisdom in Baghdad, under the Abbasid caliphs, became a center for translation, research, and the development of various sciences.

questions.text: The Muslim scientist, Al-Khawarzami, is famous for his work on:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: There is no significant evidence to suggest that Al-Khwarizmi made major contributions to the field of physics. His work was primarily focused on mathematics and astronomy, and there is little record of his involvement in the study of physical phenomena or the development of principles in physics.

Al-Khwarizmi is most famous for his work in algebra, and his name is the origin of the term "algebra." He wrote a groundbreaking book titled "Kitab al-Mukhtasar fi Hisab al-Jabr wal-Muqabala" (The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing), which systematically introduced the concept of solving linear and quadratic equations. His work laid the foundation for algebra as a distinct branch of mathematics and had a profound impact on the development of the subject in the centuries to come.

Al-Khwarizmi's contributions to chemistry were not as significant as his contributions to mathematics. While there were advancements in the field of chemistry during the Islamic Golden Age, Al-Khwarizmi himself is not particularly known for his work in chemistry. The focus of his intellectual endeavors was more on mathematical and astronomical pursuits.

Similar to physics and chemistry, there is limited evidence to suggest that Al-Khwarizmi made substantial contributions to the field of medicine. His primary contributions were in mathematics and astronomy, and there is little indication that he played a prominent role in medical research or practice.

questions.text: Fundamental ideas of modern celestial mechanics were established by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Johannes Kepler and Galileo Galilei made important contributions to astronomy, their work laid the groundwork for Newton's later developments in celestial mechanics. Kepler formulated the three laws of planetary motion, which described the elliptical orbits of planets around the sun. Galileo, through his observations with the telescope, contributed to the understanding of the phases of Venus, the moons of Jupiter, and the nature of celestial bodies. While their contributions were crucial for understanding celestial phenomena, they did not provide the comprehensive theoretical framework that Newton later developed.

Sir Isaac Newton's work, especially outlined in his monumental work "Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica" (Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy), published in 1687, established the fundamental principles of celestial mechanics. Newton formulated the laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, which explained not only how objects move on Earth but also how celestial bodies interact with each other. His laws provided a unified and comprehensive theory that explained the motion of planets, moons, and other celestial bodies in our solar system.

Robert Hooke was a contemporary of Newton and made important contributions to various scientific fields, including physics and astronomy. However, Hooke's work was not as influential as Newton's in the development of celestial mechanics. Hooke had some disputes with Newton, but his contributions were more focused on other areas, such as the elasticity of materials and the law of springs.

Tycho Brahe was an influential observational astronomer, and Halley is known for calculating the orbit of Halley's Comet. While Tycho's precise observations of celestial bodies were crucial for later astronomers, and Halley made significant contributions, they did not establish the fundamental ideas of celestial mechanics. Halley did play a role in encouraging Newton to publish his work and financed the publication of Newton's "Principia."

His laws provided a unified and comprehensive theory that explained the motion of planets, moons, and other celestial bodies in our solar system.

questions.text: The decimal system was invented by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The decimal system was invented by Indian mathematicians around the 6th century CE. The most significant figure associated with this development is the Indian mathematician and astronomer Brahmagupta. He wrote the "Brahmasphutasiddhanta," in which he introduced the decimal number system, including the concept of zero. This system, often referred to as the Hindu-Arabic numeral system, eventually spread to the Islamic world and Europe, becoming the basis for the modern numerical system used worldwide.

The ancient Greeks did not invent the decimal system. Greek mathematics, while highly influential in areas such as geometry and certain numerical concepts, primarily used a system based on letters of the alphabet to represent numbers. This system did not have the place-value system and the concept of zero that characterize the decimal system.

While the ancient Chinese made significant contributions to mathematics, including the development of various mathematical algorithms, the decimal system as we know it today did not originate in China. The Chinese traditionally used counting rods and a positional notation system, but it was distinct from the decimal system that emerged in India.

The ancient Egyptians used a counting system based on hieroglyphs and a base-10 system for some purposes, but their system lacked the place-value concept crucial to the decimal system. The Egyptian system did not have a symbol for zero, and it did not have the same mathematical sophistication as the decimal system developed later in India.

questions.text: Which of the physical quantities does not have the same units:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Impulse (denoted by J) and momentum (denoted by p) are related physical quantities. The impulse-momentum theorem states that impulse is equal to the change in momentum. Mathematically, J=Δp, where Δp is the change in momentum. The units for impulse are given by the product of force and time (N⋅s), while the units for momentum are given by the product of mass and velocity (kg⋅m/s). Therefore, impulse and momentum do have the same units.

Pressure (denoted by P) and strain are related to the deformation of materials. Pressure is defined as force per unit area (P=F/A), and its units are force divided by area (N/m)2, which is equivalent to Pascal, denoted as Pa). Strain, on the other hand, is the ratio of the change in size to the original size and is dimensionless, meaning it has no units. Therefore, pressure and strain do not have the same units.

Planck's constant (denoted by h) and angular momentum (denoted by L) are distinct physical quantities. Planck's constant is a fundamental constant in quantum mechanics and has units of energy multiplied by time (J⋅s). Angular momentum, on the other hand, has units of mass multiplied by velocity multiplied by radius (kg⋅m 2/s). Therefore, Planck's constant and angular momentum do have the same units.

Bulk modulus (denoted by K) and compressibility are related to the response of materials to changes in pressure. Bulk modulus is a measure of a substance's resistance to uniform compression, and its units are pressure (Pa). Compressibility, on the other hand, is a measure of the relative volume change of a fluid or solid subjected to a pressure change, and it is the reciprocal of bulk modulus. Compressibility is dimensionless and does have the same units as bulk modulus.

Strain, on the other hand, is the ratio of the change in size to the original size and is dimensionless, meaning it has no units. Therefore, pressure and strain do not have the same units.

questions.text: The SI unit of force is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The Newton is the SI unit of force and is defined as the force required to accelerate a one-kilogram mass at a rate of one meter per second squared. Mathematically, 1 Newton (N) is equivalent to 1 kg⋅m/s2.

The dyne is a unit of force in the CGS (centimeter-gram-second) system. It is defined as the force required to accelerate a one-gram mass at a rate of one centimeter per second squared. One dyne is equal to 10−5N. While the dyne is still used in some scientific contexts, the Newton is the preferred unit in the SI system.

The slug is a unit of mass in the English Engineering system. It is not a unit of force. The force unit in the English Engineering system is the pound-force (lbf), and it is related to the slug by the equation 1 slug⋅ft/s2=1lbf

The foot-pound is a unit of energy or work in the English Engineering system. It represents the work done when a force of one pound-force is applied over a distance of one foot. It is not a unit of force. The pound-force (lbf) is the unit of force in the English system.

questions.explanation: The Newton is the SI unit of force and is defined as the force required to accelerate a one-kilogram mass at a rate of one meter per second squared. Mathematically, 1 Newton (N) is equivalent to 1 kg⋅m/s2.

questions.text: The SI unit of energy is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The Joule is the SI unit of energy and is defined as the amount of work done when a force of one Newton acts on a point that moves one meter in the direction of the force. Mathematically, 1J=1N⋅m. The Joule is a fundamental unit in the International System of Units (SI) and is widely used to measure various forms of energy, including mechanical, thermal, and electromagnetic energy.

The calorie is a unit of energy commonly used in nutrition and thermodynamics. The most common unit is the "small calorie" (cal), which is defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of one gram of water by one degree Celsius. Another related unit is the "large calorie" or kilocalorie (kcal), which is equal to 1000 small calories. While the calorie is still used in some contexts, especially in nutrition, it is not part of the SI system.

The slug is a unit of mass in the English Engineering system. It is not a unit of energy. The pound-force (lbf) is the unit of force in the English system, and the foot-pound (ft·lbf) is the unit of energy.

The electron volt is a unit of energy commonly used in particle physics and atomic physics. It is the amount of kinetic energy gained or lost by an electron when it is accelerated through an electric potential difference of one volt. options.explanation: Fundamental units are the basic units that are independent and are not derived from other units. For example, the unit of speed is derived by dividing the unit of length by the unit of time (meters per second). questions.text: The branch of physics which deals with motion of bodies under the action of forces is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Mechanics is the correct answer. It is the branch of physics that studies the motion of objects and the forces acting upon them. Statistical Mechanics is a branch of physics that uses statistical methods to explain the behavior of a large number of particles in a system. It deals with the statistical properties of ensembles of particles, connecting macroscopic thermodynamic properties to the microscopic behavior of individual particles.

Thermodynamics is the branch of physics that deals with the relationships between heat and other forms of energy. While it is related to the study of macroscopic properties like temperature, pressure, and entropy, it doesn't specifically focus on the motion of bodies under the action of forces.

Electrostatics is a branch of physics that deals with stationary electric charges and the forces between them in the absence of motion. It is a subfield of electromagnetism but does not involve the motion of bodies under the influence of forces in the same way mechanics does.

questions.text: The Muslim scientist who wrote the books on Astronomy, Algebra and Arithmetic was:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Ibn al-Haitham, also known as Alhazen, was a Persian mathematician, astronomer, and physicist. He made significant contributions to optics and the understanding of vision. Al-Beruni, or Abu Rayhan al-Biruni, was a Persian scholar who made contributions to various fields, including astronomy, mathematics, physics, and geography. While he wrote extensively on astronomy and mathematics, he is not specifically known for his work in algebra and arithmetic.

Al-Khawarizmi, often referred to as the "father of algebra," was a Persian mathematician, astronomer, and geographer who made significant contributions to the fields of algebra, arithmetic, and astronomy. His book "Al-Kitab al-Mukhtasar fi Hisab al-Jabr wal-Muqabala" (The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing) laid the foundations for algebra and introduced systematic methods for solving linear and quadratic equations.

questions.text: The first book of physics was written by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Lord Kelvin (William Thomson) was a 19th-century Scottish physicist known for his work in thermodynamics and the development of the Kelvin temperature scale. While he made significant contributions to physics, he did not write the first book of physics.

Aristotle, a Greek philosopher, wrote a work titled "Physics" (or "Physica" in Latin). Aristotle's "Physics" is considered one of the earliest systematic treatises on natural philosophy or what we now call physics. In this work, Aristotle explored concepts related to motion, causality, and the nature of the physical world. While some of Aristotle's ideas were later revised or replaced by more accurate models, his work laid the foundation for early Western understanding of the natural world.

Michael Faraday was a 19th-century English scientist who made significant contributions to the fields of electromagnetism and electrochemistry. He is known for his experiments on electromagnetic induction and the laws of electrolysis. While Faraday made important contributions to physics, he did not write the first book of physics.

Sir Isaac Newton, a 17th-century English mathematician and physicist, wrote the "Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica" (Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy), commonly known as the "Principia." This groundbreaking work laid the foundation for classical mechanics and formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation. However, it was not the first book of physics; Aristotle's "Physics" predates Newton's "Principia."

options.explanation: Derived units are formed by combining base units through multiplication or division. They express physical quantities and can be related to one or more base units. Newton (N): Derived unit for force (mass × acceleration). Joule (J): Derived unit for energy (force × distance). Watt (W): Derived unit for power (energy / time). These derived units are combinations of base units that help express various physical quantities encountered in scientific measurements and calculations.

In the International System of Units (SI), base units are the fundamental units used to define other units. There are seven SI base units: Meter (m): Base unit for length. Kilogram (kg): Base unit for mass. Second (s): Base unit for time. Ampere (A): Base unit for electric current. Kelvin (K): Base unit for temperature. Mole (mol): Base unit for amount of substance. Candela (cd): Base unit for luminous intensity. Basic unit is another name for base unit.

questions.text: The unit of dimensions of which of the following pairs of physical quantities are identical?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Both impulse and momentum have the same unit of dimensions. Impulse is defined as force multiplied by time, while momentum is the product of mass and velocity. Both quantities share the unit of dimensions [mass] × [velocity], which is equivalent to [force] × [time], demonstrating their similarity in units. They have dimension, [MLT−1].

Stress and strain, while related, do not have identical units. Stress is force applied per unit area and has dimensions of pressure ([force] / [area]) i.e. [ML-1 T-2], whereas strain is a dimensionless quantity expressing the deformation of a material under stress, so it doesn't have a unit of dimensions.

Pressure and density also have different units. Pressure is force per unit area ([force] / [area]) i.e. [ML-1 T -2], while density is mass per unit volume ([mass] / [volume]) i.e. [ML-3]. These quantities have distinct units and dimensions.

Gravitational potential and energy have different dimensions. Gravitational potential has dimensions of [length]² / [time]² i.e. [L²T-²], while energy has dimensions of [mass]×[length]² / [time]² i.e [ML²T-2]. They aren't directly equivalent in terms of units or dimensions.

questions.text: The present international standard of time is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect according to the explanation given below.

The present international standard of time, as measured by atomic clocks, is defined by the exact number of vibrations or oscillations of the cesium atom's electrons.
